segments.purgatorio-16-g28: This segment is only a period (full stop). It carries no lexical or grammatical content itself; its function is purely punctuation to mark the end of the sentence or a pause in the text as presented in the edition.
segments.purgatorio-16-g29: 'Così' (fronted adverb) modifies the whole clause: 'thus/so'. 'per una voce' gives the means ('by a single voice'). 'detto fue' is an inversion of the expected 'fu detto' — a past passive/periphrastic construction (passive of dire), here meaning 'it was said'; the inverted order is a poetic older word order.
segments.purgatorio-16-g30: 'Onde' here functions as a connective meaning 'whereupon' / 'therefore', linking the reported speech to the next speaker. '’l' is the elided form of 'il' (the), 'maestro mio' = 'my master/teacher' (Virgil), and 'disse' is the passato remoto 'said'. The colon introduces his direct speech.
segments.purgatorio-16-g31: 'Rispondi' is a direct imperative (2nd person singular) meaning 'Answer' or 'Reply' — a command to the speaker who had been addressed; the comma shows it is part of the direct address.
segments.purgatorio-16-g32: The coordinating 'e' links a second command. 'Domanda' here is also an imperative ('ask'); 'se' introduces an indirect question meaning 'whether' (not a conditional); 'quinci' = 'from here'; 'si va' is the impersonal construction (si + verb) meaning 'one goes/is it possible to go'; 'sùe' is an archaic/poetic form meaning 'up' (destination).
segments.purgatorio-16-g33: 'E io:' simply introduces the speaker ('And I:') with 'io' as the nominative subject; the colon introduces the ensuing quoted speech.
segments.purgatorio-16-g34: The vocative 'O creatura' addresses the being directly ('O creature'). 'Che ti mondi' is a relative clause; 'che' = 'who/that' and 'ti mondi' uses the verb 'mondare' (purge/cleanse) — here presented as an ongoing/present action (Dante often uses subjunctive forms after 'che' to characterize). 'Per tornar bella' is a purpose phrase 'in order to become beautiful again' (tornar = 'become again'), and 'a colui che ti fece' places the goal 'for him who made you' with 'fece' the passato remoto 'made'.
segments.purgatorio-16-g35: Object-before-verb inversion: 'maraviglia' (a wonder/marvel) precedes the verb 'udirai' (future, 2nd person singular 'you will hear'). This poetic order emphasizes the object ('wonders you will hear').
segments.purgatorio-16-g36: 'Se' introduces a condition ('if'). 'Mi' is the clitic object pronoun 'me' placed before the verb, and 'secondi' (from secondare) is the present form used here in the sense 'you follow/assist me' — conversationally rendered 'if you follow me' or 'if you help me'. The construction is a simple conditional addressed to the listener.
segments.purgatorio-16-g37: Io (subject pronoun) + ti (clitic object) + seguiterò (future indicative, 1st person singular) = "I will follow you." quanto mi lece is an adverbial phrase: quanto = "as much as"; mi lece is an archaic impersonal or semi-impersonal construction meaning "is permitted to me" (literally "it pleases/lets me"), so the clause means "as far as I am permitted."
segments.purgatorio-16-g38: rispuose is the passato remoto (simple past) 3rd-person singular of rispondere, used here as the narrative tag "he answered." The semicolon separates the reporting clause from the reported speech that follows.
segments.purgatorio-16-g39: This clause has an if-clause introduced by se = "if." veder is an infinitive used nominally ("seeing" or "the act of seeing"). The sequence veder fummo is an archaic word order: fummo is the (archaic/past) form of essere meaning "we were," and here the line reflects inverted, poetic syntax where the idea is roughly "if seeing does not allow us" (i.e., if sight will not permit it). non is the standard negation, and lascia is present indicative of lasciare used in the sense "let/allow."
segments.purgatorio-16-g40: l'udir is the definite article + infinitive construction meaning "the hearing" or more naturally here "hearing" as a noun. ci is the clitic for "us." terrà is future indicative (3rd person singular) of tenere, meaning "will keep/hold." giunti is a past participle used predicatively with ci ("kept joined/together"). in quella vece is an adverbial phrase where vece (an archaic form of "vece/vece") means "stead/turn/place," so the whole means "hearing will keep us joined in that stead/role."
segments.purgatorio-16-g18: Quei is a demonstrative pronoun meaning 'those' placed before the verb; sono is the present indicative of essere, 'they are'; spirti is a poetic spelling of spiriti, 'spirits' (spiritual beings). The clause literally states 'Those are spirits.'
segments.purgatorio-16-g19: maestro is a vocative addressing the listener (Virgil) — 'master' or 'teacher' — set off by the preceding comma.
segments.purgatorio-16-g20: ch’ is the contracted form of che, here a relative pronoun 'who/which/that'; i is the subject pronoun 'I'; odo is the present indicative first person singular of udire/odere, 'I hear.' The whole clause is 'that I hear.'
segments.purgatorio-16-g21: This segment is punctuation (a comma) carrying over from the layout; it separates the reply from the preceding speech.
segments.purgatorio-16-g22: diss' io is a contracted/poetic form of dissi/disse io, the past (perfect/simple) of dire used as 'I said.' The apostrophe marks elision common in Dante's verse.
segments.purgatorio-16-g23: Ed = e 'and'; elli here is the subject 'he' (the speaker) but the provided sense list doesn't include that modern pronoun sense; a is the preposition 'to'; me is the indirect object 'me' ('to me'). The phrase introduces reported speech ('And he to me:').
segments.purgatorio-16-g24: Tu is the vocative/subject 'you'; vero functions adverbially as an emphatic 'truly' or 'indeed'; apprendi is present indicative second person singular of apprendere, here 'you understand/learn/find out' (an assertion addressed to the addressee).
segments.purgatorio-16-g25: e = 'and'; d’ is the elided form of di and here means 'from' or 'out of' (d'iracundia = 'from anger'); iracundia = 'anger/wrath'; van is the present third-person plural of andare used with a gerund to express a progressive or continued action ('they go' + gerund = 'they are going about'); solvendo is the gerund of solvere 'loosening/undoing'; il nodo is the direct object 'the knot.' Grammatically the phrase says that, due to anger, they are untying/loosening the knot.
segments.purgatorio-16-g26: Or = 'now' or 'then'; tu 'you' (address); chi 'who'; se’ is the archaic second-person singular of essere ('you are'); che is a relative conjunction 'who/that' introducing the clause; l' is the article 'the' (here contracted); nostro 'our'; fummo in the original line context is 'fumo' (smoke), but the provided sense list gives 'we were' — that sense does not fit here, so no provided sense applies; fendi is a present-tense verb (2nd person singular) from fendere/ fendire meaning 'you cleave/split.' The clause asks 'who are you that split our smoke?'.
segments.purgatorio-16-g27: e = 'and'; di noi = 'about/of us' (di + noi); parli is present second-person singular 'you speak' or 'you talk'; pur = 'still/even' (here reinforcing the comparison); come se introduces a hypothetical/comparative clause 'as if'; the provided sense for se (meaning 'you are') does not cover its use here as the conjunction 'if/as if', so -1 is given; tue in the manuscript functions as a form tied to the hypothetical (the exact morphological status is archaic and doesn't match the provided 'your/yours' sense, so set to -1); partissi is the imperfect subjunctive (2nd person singular) of partire meaning 'you would depart/you were to leave' in a contrary-to-fact or hypothetical sense; ancor = 'still/yet'; lo tempo = 'the time' (here 'your time' or 'the time allotted'); per calendi = 'for/by the calends' (a calendrical reference). The clause means they reproach him for speaking as if he were going to leave at the appointed time.
segments.purgatorio-16-g55: Così is an adverb meaning 'thus' or 'so' that modifies the following verb. Rispuose is the passato remoto (literary simple past) 3rd person singular of rispondere (Dante's older/poetic form rispuose), meaning 'he answered' with the subject omitted and understood from context.
segments.purgatorio-16-g56: E is the coordinating conjunction 'and', linking this verb to the next action. Soggiunse is the passato remoto 3rd person singular of soggiungere, here meaning 'he added' (introducing the reported speech that follows); the colon after it signals the direct quotation.
segments.purgatorio-16-g57: This is direct speech. I' is an archaic contraction of io ('I'). Ti is the clitic pronoun 'you' (object form). Prego is present indicative 'I beg' / 'I ask' (here used as 'I beg you'). Che introduces a subordinate clause and governs the verb in the clause. Per me = 'for me' (in my behalf). Prieghi is the subjunctive form (second person) of pregare used in a dependent purpose/request clause introduced by che — 'that you pray' (Dante's spelling). Quando introduces a temporal clause 'when'; sù is an adverb 'up'/'up above' (accented), and sarai is the future indicative 2nd person singular 'you will be'.
segments.purgatorio-16-g58: E is 'and', a simple conjunction; io is the emphatic subject pronoun 'I'; a lui is a dative phrase 'to him' (literally 'to him'), here introducing the speech directed at that person. The colon indicates the reply/quoted speech follows.
segments.purgatorio-16-g59: Per fede is an idiom 'by (my) faith' or 'upon my faith' (an oath/pledge). Mi ti lego is a reflexive/reciprocal construction meaning 'I bind myself to you' (mi = myself, ti = to you) with lego = present indicative 1st singular 'I bind/promise' (in older usage 'legare' = bind, pledge). Di introduces the infinitival clause 'di far' = 'to do'; ciò che = 'that which' / 'what'; mi chiedi is present indicative 2nd singular 'you ask me'. Together: 'By my faith I bind myself to you to do what you ask me.'
segments.purgatorio-16-g60: Ma = 'but' introducing contrast. Io scoppio is present indicative 1st person 'I burst' (used metaphorically: 'I am bursting' or 'I am overcome'), and dentro ad un dubbio is a prepositional phrase meaning 'within/into a doubt' (dentro ad = 'within/inside', an older collocation), i.e. 'I am filled/overwhelmed by a doubt.'
segments.purgatorio-16-g61: S’io is a contracted form corresponding to se io ('if I'); here the contraction is archaic (note: the lemma given is si but the form functions as 'se' = 'if'). Non negates the clause. Me ne spiego: spiego is present indicative 1st person 'I explain'; the clitics me ne mean roughly 'explain it to myself' or 'explain it of it/away' (ne = of it/about it, me = to myself). The whole conditional means 'if I do not explain it to myself' / 'unless I explain it.'
segments.purgatorio-16-g62: Prima is an adverb meaning "formerly/previously." era is the imperfect (or simple past in narrative) of essere "was." scempio is a predicate noun meaning "outrage/abuse" (a strong wrongdoing). Together: "Previously it was an outrage."
segments.purgatorio-16-g63: e = "and." ora = "now." è fatto is a resultative or passive-like construction with essere + past participle fatto = "is made/has become." doppio = "double." ne la sentenza tua = "in your sentence/judgment" (ne = "in" or "within" + the noun phrase la sentenza tua). The clause says that, in the addressee's judgment, the matter has become doubled.
segments.purgatorio-16-g64: che = relative pronoun "which/that" introducing a clause. mi = indirect object "to me" ("it makes me"). fa = present indicative of fare "makes/causes." certo = adjective/adverb "certain/convincing." qui = "here/in this matter/at this point." So: "which makes me certain/convinced here."
segments.purgatorio-16-g65: e = "and." altrove = adverb "elsewhere." This simply links the speaker's conviction to other places/contexts as well: "and elsewhere."
segments.purgatorio-16-g66: quello = "that/that thing." ov' is a contraction of ove/ov = "where" (archaic). io = emphatic subject "I." l' is the clitic article or object clitic (here standing for "it" or "him/that") attached to accoppio. accoppio is the 1st-person present indicative of accoppiare/accoppiare-like verb meaning "I pair/join together." The phrase quello ov' io l'accoppio therefore means roughly "that (place/thing) where I join it" or "that which I pair with it."
segments.purgatorio-16-g67: Lo mondo = "the world" (article + noun). è = present indicative of essere "is." ben così tutto diserto = an emphatic phrase: ben così = "indeed/so," tutto diserto = "completely deserted/barren." d'ogne virtute = contraction of di + ogne (ogni) = "of every virtue." Together: "The world is indeed so entirely deserted of every virtue."
segments.purgatorio-16-g68: come = "as/just as." tu = "you" (subject). mi = indirect object "to me" (here: "as you tell me"). sone is an archaic form of sonare or sonare-used metaphorically "you say/sound to me"; here it means "you tell me/you assert." So: "as you tell me."
segments.purgatorio-16-g69: e = "and." di malizia = "of wickedness/malice." gravido = adjective "pregnant/laden/fraught." e coverto = "and covered/concealed." The pair gravido e coverto describe the world as laden and covered with malice.
segments.purgatorio-16-g70: ma = "but." priego = present 1st-person singular of pregare in a literary sense here meaning "I beg/ I entreat" (Dante's speakers often use priego as a polite request), followed by che introducing a subordinate content clause. m' = clitic for "to me." addite is 2nd-person plural (imperative or present subjunctive used as polite imperative) of additare "point out/indicate." la cagione = "the cause/reason." The clause: "but I beg you to point out to me the cause."
segments.purgatorio-16-g71: sì ch' = "so that." i' is an abbreviated form of io used in older/poetic texts meaning "I." la = feminine object pronoun "it" (referring to la cagione). veggia is present subjunctive of veggiare/vedere used after sì che for purpose/result: "so that I may see it." e = "and." ch' i = "that I" (clipped). la mostri = present subjunctive/imperative idea: mostri = "I may show it" (verb mostrare with la = it). altrui = "to others." Overall: "so that I may see it and show it to others." Note the use of subjunctive/poetic truncation.
segments.purgatorio-16-g72: ché = conjunction "for/because/that." nel = contraction of in + il = "in the." cielo = "heaven/sky." uno = "one/one person/one (cause)." The phrase "ché nel cielo uno" = "for one [cause/answer] is in heaven."
segments.purgatorio-16-g73: e = "and." un = "one/another." qua giù = adverbial phrase "here below/on this side down." la = direct object pronoun (referring back to the cause) and pone = present indicative of porre/porre-like verb "places/sets." The clause means "and another places it here below," i.e. competing explanations are given in heaven and here below.
segments.purgatorio-16-g129: Ben is an adverb of emphasis ('well/clearly') modifying puoi; puoi is 2nd-person singular present of potere and takes the bare infinitive veder. The clause introduced by che is a declarative subordinate: la mala condotta is the subject and è is the 3rd-singular copula; la cagion begins a relative clause (che) whose verb phrase l' mondo ha fatto reo uses ha fatto (compound perfect) to mean 'has made' with reo as the predicative adjective 'guilty'.
segments.purgatorio-16-g130: e non introduces a contrast: 'and not...'. Natura here is the noun 'nature' (the provided sense list doesn't include the noun, so it's flagged accordingly). The relative clause che 'n voi sia corrotta uses the present subjunctive sia after a negation/contrast to express 'that (it) be corrupted in you'; corrotta is a past participle used adjectivally, and '’n' is the contracted form of in ('in you').
segments.purgatorio-16-g131: Soleva is the imperfect of solere, 'was wont to / used to', placed before the noun for poetic inversion; Roma is the subject (proper name). The comma signals that a relative clause about Rome follows.
segments.purgatorio-16-g132: che is a relative pronoun 'which/that' referring back to Roma; ’l buon mondo feo = 'made the good world', where buon (prepositive) qualifies mondo and feo is an archaic/past form (poetic) of fare meaning 'made' or 'formed'. The clause describes Rome's role in 'making' the good world.
segments.purgatorio-16-g133: Due is the numeral 'two'; soli here means 'suns' (a figurative plural noun), and aver is the infinitive 'to have' functioning as the complement of soleva ('used to have two suns'). The order (numeral + noun + infinitive) is a poetic object-verb construction tied to the earlier soleva.
segments.purgatorio-16-g134: che is another relative pronoun ('which'); l’una e l’altra is a correlative pair meaning 'the one and the other'; strada is 'road/path' and is the object that the verb facean refers to. Facean is the imperfect (they were making/causing) of fare (poetic form facean/facevano) and takes the infinitive vedere: 'they caused (it) to be seen' or 'they showed the way.'
segments.purgatorio-16-g135: This short phrase completes the previous clause: e del mondo e di Deo ('both to/of the world and to God'). Del is the contraction di + il and di before Deo marks the second coordinate; the two prepositional phrases indicate the beneficiaries/realms for which the 'suns' showed the way.
segments.purgatorio-16-g136: 'L’un l’altro ha spento' is an idiomatic inversion meaning 'one has extinguished/suppressed the other.' 'L’' is the elided article, the first 'un' is the subject notion 'one', 'l’altro' is the direct object 'the other', and 'ha spento' is perfect (avere + past participle) 'has extinguished/put out.'
segments.purgatorio-16-g137: 'ed è giunta' is compound perfect with auxiliary essere plus past participle 'giunta' (feminine singular to agree with 'la spada'): 'and the sword has come/arrived.' 'col pasturale' = 'with the pastoral staff' (col = con + il).
segments.purgatorio-16-g138: The phrase 'e l’un con l’altro insieme' = 'and the one with the other together.' 'Per viva forza mal convien che vada' contains the impersonal construction 'convien che' + subjunctive ('vada'): 'it is (ill-)fitting / it is wrong that they should go on by live/actual force.' 'viva forza' = 'actual, living force' (i.e. sheer force), and 'mal' negates the fitness ('ill-fitting').
segments.purgatorio-16-g139: 'Però che' = 'because/for' introduces the causal clause that follows (explaining why going together by force is ill-fitting).
segments.purgatorio-16-g140: 'giunti' is a past participle used adverbially here: 'once joined' or 'having been joined' (plural), referring back to the two powers becoming united.
segments.purgatorio-16-g141: 'l’un l’altro non teme' = 'one does not fear the other.' Same idiomatic inversion as before; 'non teme' is present indicative 3rd person singular ('does not fear').
segments.purgatorio-16-g142: 'Se non mi credi' = 'If you do not believe me.' 'Se' introduces a conditional; 'credi' is present indicative 2nd person singular 'you believe', and 'mi' is the object pronoun 'me.'
segments.purgatorio-16-g143: 'Pon mente a la spiga' is an imperative instruction: 'put (your) mind to / pay attention to the ear (of grain).' 'Pon' is the (imperative) form of porre 'put', 'mente' = 'mind/attention', and 'a la spiga' = 'to the ear (of corn)'.
segments.purgatorio-16-g144: 'Ch’ogn’ erba si conosce per lo seme' = 'for every plant is known by its seed.' 'Ch’' = 'che' introducing a statement of fact; 'ogn’ erba' = 'every herb/plant' (ognuno- contracted), 'si conosce' is reflexive/passive-like 'is known/recognised', and 'per lo seme' = 'by/by means of the seed.'
segments.purgatorio-16-g145: 'In sul paese' is an archaic locative ('in the country/land'); 'ch’Adice e Po riga' is a relative clause 'which the Adige and the Po ...' Here 'Adice' and 'Po' are river names; the verb 'riga' is used archaically to mean 'water/mark/line' or 'run through' and is figurative of the rivers crossing/irrigating the land.
segments.purgatorio-16-g146: 'Solea ... trovarsi' is an impersonal/defective past habitual: 'used to be found.' 'valore e cortesia' = 'valour and courtesy' (valor = martial or moral worth; cortesia = courtesy/chivalry), and 'trovarsi' is reflexive/intransitive 'to be found/to be present.'
segments.purgatorio-16-g147: 'Prima che Federigo avesse briga' = 'before Federigo had trouble.' 'Prima che' takes the imperfect subjunctive 'avesse' here because the main clause is past-habitual ('solea'), and 'briga' = 'trouble/quarrel.'
segments.purgatorio-16-g148: 'Or può sicuramente indi passarsi' = 'now one can certainly pass away/from there.' 'Or' = 'now', 'può' = 'can', 'sicuramente' = 'certainly'; 'indi' = 'thence/from there' (archaic/poetic). 'Passarsi' is reflexive 'to pass by/go away.'
segments.purgatorio-16-g149: 'Per qualunque lasciasse, per vergogna di ragionar coi buoni o d’appressarsi' explains the reason: 'for whoever might have left (it), out of shame to converse with the good or to approach them.' 'Per qualunque lasciasse' uses 'lasciasse' (imperfect subjunctive) in a general/conditional sense; 'per vergogna di' = 'for shame of', 'ragionar' = infinitive 'to speak', 'coi buoni' = 'with the good (men)', 'd’appressarsi' = 'to draw near' (reflexive infinitive).
segments.purgatorio-16-g1: A long noun phrase that stacks genitives and modifiers: 'Buio d’inferno' = 'darkness of hell'; 'e di notte privata d’ogne pianeto' = 'and of night deprived of every planet' (elision d’ = di). 'Privata' agrees with 'notte' (feminine). 'Ogne' is the archaic form of 'ogni'.
segments.purgatorio-16-g2: Prepositional phrase 'sotto pover cielo' = 'under a poor/wretched sky'. 'Pover' is the apocopic (truncated) adjective used poetically before 'cielo'.
segments.purgatorio-16-g3: 'quant’ esser può' is an elided form of 'quanto esser può' meaning 'as much as can be' (quant’ = quanto). It uses the infinitive 'esser' with the present 'può' to express potential degree; 'di nuvol tenebrata' modifies it: 'of cloud darkened.'
segments.purgatorio-16-g4: Main clause with negation: 'non fece al viso mio sì grosso velo' — 'did not make so thick a veil for my face'. 'Fece' is passato remoto of 'fare' (narrative past). The comparative clause 'come quel fummo ch’ivi ci coperse' = 'as that smoke which there covered us'; note elisions (ch’ = che, ivi = there).
segments.purgatorio-16-g5: 'né a sentir di così aspro pelo' continues the thought: 'nor, in feeling such rough hair' — 'a sentir' is a verbal phrase meaning 'in perceiving' or 'to feel', and 'di' introduces the quality 'of such harsh hair'.
segments.purgatorio-16-g6: Result clause introduced by 'che': 'che l’occhio stare aperto non sofferse' = 'so that the eye could not endure to remain open.' 'Stare aperto' = 'to stay/remain open'; 'sofferse' is passato remoto of 'soffrire' meaning 'to endure/sustain' (here negative = could not keep open).
segments.purgatorio-16-g7: 'Onde' here = 'wherefore/so' (introducing the consequence). 'La scorta mia saputa e fida' = 'my escort, experienced/knowing and faithful' (two adjectives after noun). 'Mi s’accostò' = 'drew near me' (passato remoto, reflexive si), and 'l’omero m’offerse' = 'he offered me his shoulder' ('m’' = to me; 'omero' = shoulder).
segments.purgatorio-16-g8: Simile introduced by 'Sì come' = 'just as'. 'Cieco va dietro a sua guida' = 'a blind man goes behind his guide'; the infinitives 'per non smarrirsi' and 'per non dar di cozzo' state purpose/avoidance ('so as not to get lost' / 'so as not to give a bump'), and the relative 'in cosa che ’l molesti' = 'into something that would trouble/annoy him' (’l = lo).
segments.purgatorio-16-g9: 'o forse ancida' is a brief parenthetical: 'or perhaps kills him.' 'Ancida' is an archaic/uncertain form here (textual/interpretive difficulties arise in medieval diction); the phrase adds the more extreme possibility (death) to 'molesti'.
segments.purgatorio-16-g10: 'M’andava io' (clitic + verb + subject pronoun for emphasis) uses the imperfect 'andava' = 'I was going' or 'I went on' through 'l’aere amaro e sozzo' = 'the bitter and filthy air'. The order m’andava io is emphatic/poetic.
segments.purgatorio-16-g11: 'Ascoltando' is a gerund meaning 'while listening/heeding'; 'il mio duca che diceva pur' = 'my duke who kept saying still/continually' — 'pur' here reinforces 'still/just'.
segments.purgatorio-16-g12: Direct quotation: 'Guarda che da me tu non sia mozzo.' 'Guarda' = 'beware/look' used to warn; 'che' introduces a subordinate clause; 'sia' is present subjunctive of 'essere' used after a warning ('that you be not...'); 'mozzo' literally 'cut off' or 'severed', here figuratively 'severed from me' (fall behind or be separated/killed).
segments.purgatorio-16-g13: 'Io sentia voci' — simple clause with 'sentia' in the imperfect meaning 'I heard' or 'I was hearing', marking an ongoing past perception; 'voci' = 'voices'.
segments.purgatorio-16-g14: Complex clause: 'e ciascuna pareva pregar' = 'and each seemed to pray/entreat'; 'per pace e per misericordia' = 'for peace and for mercy'; 'l’Agnel di Dio che le peccata leva' = 'the Lamb of God who lifts/removes their sins' — note 'Agnel' is the Italianized Latin 'Agnus' (archaic spelling) and 'leva' is present of 'levare' used in the sense 'takes away'.
segments.purgatorio-16-g15: 'Pur' = 'still/even so'; the Latin phrase 'Agnus Dei' is quoted as the opening formula ('eran le loro essordia' = 'were their beginnings/openings'). 'Eran' is imperfect 'were', and 'essordia' is an archaic plural noun meaning 'openings' or 'first words'.
segments.purgatorio-16-g16: 'Una parola in tutte era e un modo' = 'one word was in all, and one manner' — marked, somewhat inverted word order (verb after subject), stating that every voice used the same word and tone/manner.
segments.purgatorio-16-g17: 'Sì che parea tra esse ogne concordia' = 'so that there seemed among them every concord' — 'sì che' introduces the result clause; 'parea' imperfect of 'parere' = 'seemed'; 'tra esse' = 'among them' (esse = feminine pronoun referring to the voices), and 'ogne concordia' = 'complete/whole concord'.
segments.purgatorio-16-g74: Alto modifies sospir and here is used figuratively: alto = 'deep/long' rather than literally 'high' or 'tall'. Sospir is a masculine noun 'sigh'; the phrase is a nominative noun phrase standing alone as the thing that follows.
segments.purgatorio-16-g75: Che is a relative pronoun ('which/that') referring back to 'Alto sospir'. Duolo is the subject of the verb (grief). Strinse is the passato remoto (simple past) of stringere used here in the sense 'pressed/forced' (caused to tighten); in + interjection indicates the sound into which the grief was compressed — 'into "uhi!"'.
segments.purgatorio-16-g76: This segment is punctuation (a comma) separating the previous clause from what follows.
segments.purgatorio-16-g77: Mise is an archaic/special past form meaning 'put/uttered' (past of mettere) — here 'he put forth/uttered'. Fuor is an adverb 'out' and prima is an adverb 'first' (indicating that this was done before what follows).
segments.purgatorio-16-g78: E poi cominciò is a simple sequence: e = 'and', poi = 'then', cominciò is passato remoto 'he began' (introducing the speech that follows). The colon marks the start of direct speech.
segments.purgatorio-16-g79: Frate is a vocative address 'Brother' (used to address a fellow monk or brother); the comma and opening quote show direct speech begins here.
segments.purgatorio-16-g80: Lo is the archaic definite article 'the'. Mondo here means the world/humankind ('the world' as society). È is the present indicative of essere ('is'). Cieco is an adjective 'blind' used metaphorically of the world.
segments.purgatorio-16-g81: E = 'and'. Tu is the personal pronoun 'you' (subject). Vien is present indicative of venire here meaning 'come' or 'are from' (you come from/are of it). Ben is an adverb intensifying the statement ('indeed/well'). Da + lui = 'from him/it' (lui = 'him/it', referring back to 'the world').
segments.purgatorio-16-g82: Voi 'you' (plural) addresses the hearers. Che introduces a relative clause 'who'. Vivete is present indicative 'you live'. Ogne cagion 'every cause' is the object. Recat e here is 2nd-person plural imperative/ hortative ('bring' / 'carry' — an instruction addressed to you). Pur = 'even/still'. Suso is an adverb meaning 'upward' (to the sky); note the metadata given for 'suso' is inconsistent, so sense index is not available (see -1). Al + cielo = 'to heaven'.
segments.purgatorio-16-g83: Pur 'even/still'. Come se = 'as if' (come = 'as'; se = 'if/whether' in the conventional 'as if' construction — the provided sense for 'se' is incorrect so index is -1). Tutto 'everything/all'. Movesse is the imperfect subjunctive of muovere used after 'come se' to present a hypothetical ('were moved'). Seco = 'with it/him' (i.e. along with that heaven), and di necessitate = 'by necessity' (indicating compulsion); movesse + se co + di necessitate expresses 'as if everything were moved along by necessity.'
segments.purgatorio-16-g84: Se così fosse is the protasis of a counterfactual conditional: se = 'if' (the provided sense entry is incorrect, so -1), così = 'thus/so', fosse is the imperfect subjunctive of essere ('were' / 'were so'), used to introduce an unreal/hypothetical situation.
segments.purgatorio-16-g85: In voi = 'in you' (i.e. within you/among you). Fora is an archaic conditional form meaning 'would be' (from essere); the provided senses for 'fora' refer to a different lemma, so index is -1. Distrutto is a past participle used predicatively 'destroyed'. Libero arbitrio = 'free will' (libero = 'free', arbitrio = 'freedom of choice/will').
segments.purgatorio-16-g86: E non fora giustizia = 'and there would be no justice' (non = negation; fora = archaic conditional 'would be' — sense metadata mismatches so -1). Giustizia here refers to moral/divine justice. Per ben letizia: per = 'for/in the case of', ben = 'good', letizia = 'joy' — the clause states what the consequences would be (for good, joy).
segments.purgatorio-16-g87: E per male aver lutto = 'and for evil, to have mourning' — e = 'and'; per = 'for'; male = 'evil/bad deeds'; aver = infinitive 'to have' (here used elliptically to state the consequence); lutto = 'mourning/grief'. The clause completes the pair contrasting the outcomes for good and evil.
segments.purgatorio-16-g177: Vedi is the 2nd person singular imperative (or present indicative used as a command) meaning 'look' or 'see'. l’albor is the definite article + noun 'dawn'. che is a relative pronoun introducing the following subordinate clause. per + lo fummo = 'through the smoke' (lo fummo is an older/poetic form for 'the smoke'); raia is a poetic 3rd-person singular present of a verb (raiare/raia) meaning 'sends rays' or 'casts', and it governs the infinitive biancheggiare as the result or effect: 'sends [it] to whiten' — già is the adverb 'already' and biancheggiare is the infinitive 'to whiten/become white', together meaning 'already begins to whiten'.
segments.purgatorio-16-g178: e is the coordinating conjunction 'and'. me is a dative pronoun 'to me' used with the impersonal verb convien (convien = 'it is necessary'); partirmi is the infinitive 'to leave' with the enclitic reflexive/oblique pronoun -mi attached (here functioning as the complement of convien: 'it is necessary that I leave'). The parenthesis (l’angelo è ivi) is an inserted clause 'the angel is there' (è is 'is' with locative sense, ivi 'there'). prima ch’io li paia uses prima che + present subjunctive (paia, 1st sing. subj. of parere) to mean 'before I appear', and li is an older unstressed object pronoun referring back to the angel ('to him' / 'before I appear to him').
segments.purgatorio-16-g179: Così is an adverb meaning 'thus' or 'so'. tornò is the passato remoto (simple past) 3rd-person singular of tornare, 'he returned' or 'he turned back/away'; the subject is implied from context.
segments.purgatorio-16-g180: e is 'and'. più non is the idiomatic combination meaning 'no longer' or 'no more' (più here = 'any longer' with the negative non). volle is the passato remoto 3rd-person singular of volere; with negation it conveys 'he would not' or 'he refused to'. udirmi is the infinitive udire 'to hear' with the enclitic -mi 'me' attached, so volle udirmi = 'he refused to hear me'.
segments.purgatorio-16-g112: "Di picciol bene in pria sente sapore;" is ordered poetically: Di + noun = "a taste of" (lit. "of small good"); "in pria" is an adverbial phrase meaning "at first/beforehand"; "sente sapore" is present indicative, 3rd person singular of sentire used here as "tastes/experiences the flavour". The adjective-noun order "picciol bene" = "little good" (a small or slight good).
segments.purgatorio-16-g113: "quivi s’inganna," — "quivi" = "there/at that point"; "s' inganna" is a pronominal (reflexive) verb construction here meaning "he deceives himself / is mistaken." It's present indicative, 3rd person singular; the reflexive pronoun «s'» makes the action reflexive rather than transitive.
segments.purgatorio-16-g114: "e dietro ad esso corre," — "e" = "and"; "dietro ad" = "after/behind"; "esso" is a neuter pronoun "it" (referring back to the little good); "corre" is present indicative 3rd person singular of correre and here means "runs after/ hastens after." The phrase means "and he runs after it."
segments.purgatorio-16-g115: "se guida o fren non torce suo amore." — "se" here = "if" (introducing the conditional negative clause); "guida" = "guide" and "fren" = "bridle/restraint" are the possible agents that can check desire; "non torce" = present indicative negative "does not turn/twist aside" (torcere used figuratively); "suo amore" = "his/its love/desire." The whole clause: "if no guide or bridle turns aside his love."
segments.purgatorio-16-g116: "Onde convenne legge per fren porre;" — "Onde" = "therefore/wherefore" introducing the consequence; "convenne" is a past impersonal/defective form meaning "it was necessary/one had to"; "legge" here functions as "law(s)" (poetic noun); "per fren porre" literally "to put (porre) for/as a bridle" — "porre" = infinitive "to put/establish." The clause means: "Therefore it became necessary to set laws as a bridle/restraint."
segments.purgatorio-16-g117: "convenne rege aver," — repetition of the impersonal "convenne" = "it was necessary/it was fitting"; "rege" = "a king/ruler" (accusative after the impersonal construction is typical of older Italian), and "aver" = infinitive "to have." Together: "it was necessary to have a king."
segments.purgatorio-16-g118: "che discernesse\nde la vera cittade almen la torre." — "che" = relative "who/that" introducing what the king must do; "discernesse" is imperfect subjunctive used in a relative clause of purpose/requirement meaning "might distinguish/discern"; "de la vera cittade" = "of/from the true city"; "almen" = "at least"; "la torre" must be read as the noun "the tower" in this construction (poetic word order places the object at the end). The sense: "who could at least distinguish the tower of/from the true city."
segments.purgatorio-16-g119: "Le leggi son," — simple declarative clause: "Le" = definite article "the"; "leggi" = plural noun "laws"; "son" = contracted form of "sono," present indicative plural of essere, "are." It means "The laws are (in place)."
segments.purgatorio-16-g120: "ma chi pon mano ad esse?" — "ma" = "but"; "chi" = indefinite/interrogative "who?"; "pon" is an archaic present form of porre (3rd sing.), here meaning "puts/places"; "mano" = "hand" (idiomatically "to put one's hand to" = to take action); "ad esse" = "to them" ("esse" = neuter pronoun referring back to "le leggi"). The whole question: "But who will put a hand to them?" i.e., who will enforce them?
segments.purgatorio-16-g121: 'Nullo' is an emphatic, archaic negative pronoun meaning 'no one' or 'none' used alone as the sentence's subject; the following comma marks a pause before the explanatory clause.
segments.purgatorio-16-g122: Però che = 'for/because' introducing a causal clause. '’l' is the elided definite article (il), 'pastor' = 'shepherd', and the relative 'che procede' is a present indicative relative clause ('who proceeds/goes on') modifying 'pastor'.
segments.purgatorio-16-g123: 'rugumar' is an infinitive meaning 'to grumble/mutter' and here is used with the modal verb 'può' (present indicative 3rd person singular) to mean 'may/can grumble'. The comma separates this clause from the contrasting clause that follows.
segments.purgatorio-16-g124: Contrastive 'ma' = 'but'; 'non ha' is present indicative 'does not have'; 'l’unghie' (article + noun) is plural and 'fesse' (adjective/past participle) agrees in number: 'split'—so 'he has no split nails/hoofs' (i.e. no cloven claws).
segments.purgatorio-16-g125: 'Per che' here means 'wherefore/therefore' introducing the result; 'la gente' is the subject of what follows ('the people').
segments.purgatorio-16-g126: The relative 'che' ('who') links to 'la gente'. 'sua guida' = 'their guide' (sua agrees with gente); 'vede' is present indicative 'see'. 'pur a quel ben fedire' = 'merely/only towards that good of striking' — 'pur' = 'merely', 'a' = 'to', 'quel ben' = 'that good/benefit/goal', 'fedire' (infinitive) = 'to strike/aim at'. 'ond’' (= 'onde') = 'of which/whence'; 'ella è ghiotta' = 'she is greedy for it' (here 'ella' refers back to 'gente' as a gendered collective).
segments.purgatorio-16-g127: 'Di quel si pasce' = 'feeds on that.' 'Di quel' = 'of that'; 'si pasce' is reflexive present (third singular) meaning 'feeds itself/feeds on it' — note that 'gente' as a collective is treated as singular, thus the singular verb.
segments.purgatorio-16-g128: 'e più oltre non chiede' = 'and asks no more/does not seek anything further.' 'più ... non' = 'no longer/anymore', 'oltre' = 'further', and 'chiede' is present indicative 'asks/seeks'.
segments.purgatorio-16-g166: A single punctuation mark (period) standing alone as a line/speech break; there are no lexical items to analyze.
segments.purgatorio-16-g167: "O" here is the correlative 'either' in the pair 'O ... o ...' (not an exclamation). "tuo parlar" is a noun phrase where the infinitive parlar functions as a substantive—"your speech." The clitic m' (mi) is attached to the verb inganna: m'inganna = inganna + mi = 'deceives me.'
segments.purgatorio-16-g168: "o" is the correlative 'or' completing the either/or pair. "el" is the archaic pronoun/article referring back to the preceding idea (the speech) and here functions as 'it' (or 'he'); "mi" is the clitic object 'me'; "tenta" is 3rd-person singular present of tentare meaning 'tests/tries/tempts' and takes the clitic object.
segments.purgatorio-16-g169: Rispuose is the passato remoto (simple past) of rispondere meaning 'answered' with the agent understood ('he answered'). "a me" is the indirect object 'to me.'
segments.purgatorio-16-g170: Ché is the causal conjunction 'because/for' introducing the reason clause that follows; here it begins the explanation for the previous statement.
segments.purgatorio-16-g171: Parlandomi is the gerund/participle (parlando) with the clitic mi attached — literally 'speaking to me' or 'addressing me.' Tosco is an adjective meaning 'Tuscan' (i.e. the Tuscan dialect); word order places the manner ('speaking Tuscan to me') after the clause marker.
segments.purgatorio-16-g172: Par is poetic present of parere 'to seem' (it seems). Che introduces the subordinate clause and is followed by the subjunctive senta (see note). "del buon Gherardo" = 'of/about the good Gherardo'; nulla is the negating pronoun 'nothing.' Senta is subjunctive (sentire) used after par che to express what appears to be the case: 'it seems that he hears/perceives nothing.'
segments.purgatorio-16-g173: Per + noun here means 'by' or 'under' (a surname): 'by another surname.' Sopranome is 'surname/nickname.' Nol is the archaic contracted form of non + lo = 'do not him' (i.e. 'do not know him'), placed before the verb; conosco is 1st person present 'I know.'
segments.purgatorio-16-g174: S'io is the contracted poetic form of se io ('if I' / 'unless I'). Nol again = non + lo ('not him'). Togliessi is the imperfect subjunctive of togliere ('I were to take away' / 'I would take away'), used in the protasis of a contrary-to-fact/conditional sense. Da sua figlia Gaia indicates the origin/possession ('from his daughter Gaia'); the exact referent is ambiguous, so the clause gives a hypothetical condition meaning 'unless I were to take him away from his daughter Gaia.'
segments.purgatorio-16-g175: Dio + subjunctive sia forms an optative blessing 'May God be with you.' Con voi ('with you') uses con + voi; the subjunctive expresses a wish rather than a statement of fact.
segments.purgatorio-16-g176: Ché introduces the reason/explanation 'for.' Più non ... means 'no longer' (più = 'any longer' with negation). Vegno is 1st-person present of venire used here in the sense 'I come' or 'I accompany'; vosco is an archaic form meaning 'with you' (equivalent to con voi). The whole line states that the speaker will no longer go along with them.
segments.purgatorio-16-g88: Lo is the archaic/poetic definite article 'the' placed before cielo; the order 'Lo cielo i vostri movimenti inizia' puts the subject (the heavens) before the object (your movements) and uses inizia (present indicative) to mean 'begins' or 'sets in motion.'
segments.purgatorio-16-g89: Non dico tutti is a simple negated clause: non + present indicative dico ('I say') followed by tutti ('all'), so 'I do not say all.' The short, abrupt phrasing reflects Dante's concessive tone.
segments.purgatorio-16-g90: Ma is the coordinating conjunction 'but,' marking contrast with the previous negation; it stands alone as an interjection-like connective.
segments.purgatorio-16-g91: Posto ch’i’ ’l dica is a concessive formula: 'posto che' = 'given/assumed that'; posto here functions idiomatically ('given/assuming'), ch’ = che ('that'), i = io ('I'), and dica is present subjunctive of dire used in a concessive sense ('I should say' / 'I say, even if...').
segments.purgatorio-16-g92: Lume v’è dato is passive-periphrastic: lume ('light' or 'illumination') + v’ (clitic vi = 'to you') + è (present indicative of essere) + dato (past participle of dare) = 'a light is given to you.' The phrase 'a bene e a malizia' uses a + noun to indicate 'for good and for wickedness' (moral discriminations).
segments.purgatorio-16-g93: E libero voler is a terse nominal phrase meaning 'and free will'; libero is an adjective 'free' modifying the abstract noun voler (infinitive used as noun, 'will').
segments.purgatorio-16-g94: Che introduces an explanatory/casual clause ('for/that') linking the previous statement about free will to the following conditional remark.
segments.purgatorio-16-g95: Se fatica ne le prime battaglie col ciel dura is a conditional clause: se ('if') + fatica ('toil/effort') + ne (in) + le prime battaglie ('the first battles') + col (con + il, 'with the') + ciel ('heaven/celestial sphere') + dura. Here dura is used in sense of 'lasts' ('endures') though its lemma listing shows adjectival senses; syntactically, the clause says 'if the toil endures in the first battles with Heaven.'
segments.purgatorio-16-g96: Poi vince tutto is a short main clause: poi ('then/afterwards') + vince (present indicative of vincere, 'overcomes/wins') + tutto ('everything/all'), meaning 'then everything prevails/overcomes.'
segments.purgatorio-16-g97: Se ben si notrica is a final conditional: se ben ('if indeed' / 'if well') + si (impersonal/reflexive marker) + notrica (present subj./indicative of notricare used impersonally) meaning 'if it is well nourished/fostered.' The impersonal si turns the verb into 'is nourished' rather than 'you nourish yourselves.'
segments.purgatorio-16-g98: A maggior forza e a miglior natura puts two ablative/benefactive phrases in front ('by greater force and by better nature') and follows with the finite verb soggiacete (second person plural present of soggiacere) meaning 'you are subject to' or 'you lie under' — liberi is an adverbial adjective here placed before the verb group to emphasize 'free' despite being 'subject to' in a physical/ontological sense.
segments.purgatorio-16-g99: E quella cria la mente in voi is a coordinated clause: e ('and') + quella ('that one' = that power/nature) + cria (present of criare/creare, 'creates/begets') + la mente ('the mind') + in voi ('in you'). Word order places the subject (quella) before the verb and object for emphasis on the agent.
segments.purgatorio-16-g100: Che ’l ciel non ha in sua cura is a relative clause introduced by che ('which/that'): 'which the heaven does not have in its care.' The clitic article l' before ciel is the definite article, and the verb ha is present indicative 'has' taking in sua cura ('in its care') as complement.
segments.purgatorio-16-g101: Però is a discourse marker meaning 'therefore' or 'then,' signaling consequence from the previous assertions.
segments.purgatorio-16-g102: Se ’l mondo presente disvia is a conditional: se ('if') + l' mondo presente ('the present world') + disvia (present indicative of disviare, 'goes astray' or 'departs from its course'). The verb is active and describes the world erring.
segments.purgatorio-16-g103: In voi è la cagione is an existential/assertive clause with inverted order for emphasis: 'In you is the cause' (i.e., 'the cause lies in you'). È is present indicative of essere.
segments.purgatorio-16-g104: In voi si cheggia continues the idea: in voi ('in you') + si (impersonal/reflexive marker) + cheggia (present of cheggiare meaning 'leans/ inclines'), so 'it inclines toward you' — cheggia takes an impersonal si to indicate the tendency rests in 'you.'
segments.purgatorio-16-g105: E io te ne sarò or vera spia is literal: e ('and') + io ('I') + te ('to you') + ne ('of it/about it') + sarò (future of essere, 'will be') + or ('now') + vera ('true') + spia ('spy/informer'). The sequence 'te ne sarò' means 'I will be to you about it' — 'I will inform you of it.'
segments.purgatorio-16-g106: Esce di mano a lui che la vagheggia prima che sia: Esce (present of uscire) 'comes forth/ issues' + di mano a lui ('from the hand of him') + che la vagheggia ('who longs for it/ desires it') + prima che sia (subjunctive sia, 'before it is/exists'). The relative clause uses the subjunctive after prima che.
segments.purgatorio-16-g107: A guisa di fanciulla che piangendo e ridendo pargoleggia is a simile: a guisa di ('in the manner of') + fanciulla ('a girl/young maiden') + che piangendo e ridendo ('who, crying and laughing') + pargoleggia (present 'acts like a little child' or 'dotes/behaves childishly'). The gerunds piangendo/ridendo describe simultaneous actions.
segments.purgatorio-16-g108: L’anima semplicetta che sa nulla: l' anima ('the soul') + semplicetta (diminutive/adjective 'simple little' — here affectionate/simple-minded) + che sa nulla ('which knows nothing'). The verb sa is present indicative of sapere.
segments.purgatorio-16-g109: Salvo che is a short concessive/exceptive phrase: salvo ('except') + che ('that'), introducing the qualification that follows.
segments.purgatorio-16-g110: Mossa da lieto fattore is an adjectival/participial phrase: mossa (past participle 'moved' — here passive) + da ('by') + lieto ('cheerful/joyful') + fattore ('Maker/Agent'). It means 'moved by a joyful Creator/Agent.'
segments.purgatorio-16-g111: Volontier torna a ciò che la trastulla is the concluding clause: volontier ('willingly') + torna (present of tornare, 'returns/goes back') + a ciò che ('to that which') + la (the soul; object clitic) + trastulla (present of trastullare, 'amuses/entertains'). The infinitive-like noun phrase from antes is resumed by ritorna a ciò che la trastulla.
segments.purgatorio-16-g41: Allora is an adverb meaning "then" linking this speech to the prior moment; incominciai is passato remoto (1st person singular) of incominciare, meaning "I began."
segments.purgatorio-16-g42: Con introduces an accompaniment or circumstance ("with/wearing"); quella fascia is a noun phrase "that sash/band"; che begins a relative clause modifying fascia; la morte is the subject of that relative clause; dissolve is 3rd-person singular present (Dante's present used narratively) meaning "dissolves/releases"; men is an archaic adverb (= meno) meaning "less" and modifies the following verb; vo is 1st-person singular present "I go"; suso is an adverb "up" placed after the verb for poetic word-order.
segments.purgatorio-16-g43: e is the coordinating conjunction "and"; venni is a past tense (passato remoto) meaning "I came" (here Dante narrates his arrival); qui is an adverb "here"; per introduces cause or motive ("through/because of"); l' infernale ambascia is the noun phrase "the infernal anguish/distress."
segments.purgatorio-16-g44: E se = "And if" (se introduces a conditional); Dio m'ha in sua grazia rinchiuso is a present-perfect construction (ha + past participle) meaning "God has shut/kept me in his grace," with m' as the clitic object "me" and rinchiuso as the past participle functioning as the main verb.
segments.purgatorio-16-g45: tanto che introduces a result clause "so that"; vuol is present of volere "wants/commands" and governs the subordinate clause ch’i' veggia, where veggia is the present subjunctive of vedere (vuol che + subjunctive is the standard sequence); la sua corte is the object of veggia; per modo tutto fuor del moderno uso is an adverbial phrase "in a manner wholly outside modern custom," with fuor = "outside" placed for emphasis.
segments.purgatorio-16-g46: non mi celar is a negative imperative phrase meaning "do not hide from me" (celar used in the imperative/infinitival form common in medieval/poetic Italian); chi fosti anzi la morte is a content question 'who you were before death', with anzi = "before" as a temporal adverb and fosti an archaic past form used here to mean "you were."
segments.purgatorio-16-g47: ma = "but/really"; dilmi is a contracted imperative form equivalent to dimmi (imperative of dire + mi), meaning "tell me."
segments.purgatorio-16-g48: e dimmi = "and tell me" (dimmi is imperative of dire + clitic mi); s'i' is a contraction of se + i' ("if I"), with s' representing the conditional particle "if"; vo is present of andare "I go" (here meaning "I proceed/advance"), bene = "well" and al varco = "at the pass/passage," so the clause asks "and tell me if I am going well at the pass."
segments.purgatorio-16-g49: e = "and"; tue parole is the nominative subject "your words"; fier here is an archaic/poetic form meaning "will be" (future or purposive sense: "shall be/make") linking subject to the predicate; le nostre scorte = "our escorts/escorts/guides," the predicate nominative. The line means "and your words shall be our guides."
segments.purgatorio-16-g50: Lombardo is a predicative nominal meaning "a Lombard" (person from Lombardy); fui is passato remoto (1st person singular) of essere, "I was," giving a compact subject-predicate inversion typical of poetic Italian.
segments.purgatorio-16-g51: e = "and"; fu' (fu) is passato remoto 3rd person singular of essere used here in a passive periphrasis; chiamato is the past participle "called/named," so fu' chiamato Marco = "he was called Marco" (or "I was called Marco," with the subject implicit in context).
segments.purgatorio-16-g52: del mondo = "of the world" (del = di + il); seppi would be passato remoto of sapere meaning "I knew" or "I learned," used here to state worldly knowledge/experience in a concise, narrative past.
segments.purgatorio-16-g53: e quel valore amai = "and I loved that valor" (amai = passato remoto 'I loved'); al quale introduces a relative clause "to which"; ha or ciascun disteso l'arco is a present construction where ha (present of avere) + past participle disteso yields a perfect sense "has stretched"—literally "to which now each has stretched the bow," i.e. the valor to which every man now directs his bow.
segments.purgatorio-16-g54: Per montar sù is a purposive phrase: per + infinitive = "in order to/for climbing up"; dirittamente is an adverb "straight/straightforwardly" modifying the verb; vai is 2nd-person singular present or imperative of andare and here functions as an instruction "go" (i.e., "go straight up").
segments.purgatorio-16-g150: Ben v’èn = contraction of 'ben' + 'vi è(n)' (archaic spelling) and works as an emphatic existential: 'there are indeed'. 'tre vecchi ancora' = 'three old men still' (vecchi is plural noun 'old men'). 'in cui' = 'in whom/among whom' (relative pronoun introducing a clause). 'rampogna' here is a finite verb (3rd person singular) meaning 'rebukes' with the antique word-order l’antica età (subject) following the verb for poetic effect and la nova (direct object) placed after it.
segments.purgatorio-16-g151: 'e par lor tardo' = 'and it seems slow to them': 'par' is impersonal/implicative 'seems/appears', 'lor' is the indirect object 'to them', and 'tardo' is an adjectival predicate meaning 'slow/late'. The following 'che' introduces the subordinate clause ('that God ...'), and 'ripogna' is a present (archaic) verb form meaning 'restore/put back' (3rd person) governing the object pronoun 'li' (them). 'a miglior vita' is the indirect prepositional phrase 'to a better life'.
segments.purgatorio-16-g152: A simple list of names introduced as the three persons just mentioned. 'Currado da Palazzo' and 'Guido da Castel' use 'da' as a toponymic/locative particle 'of/from (Palazzo/Castel)'; '’l buon Gherardo' = 'the good Gherardo' (article + adjective before the proper name as a laudatory epithet).
segments.purgatorio-16-g153: 'che mei si noma' — 'che' = 'who/which/that' introduces a relative clause; 'mei' here is an archaic/poetic form meaning 'once' or 'formerly' (compare modern 'mai' or 'un tempo'); 'si noma' is a reflexive/impersonal passive construction meaning 'is called' or 'was called' (si + verb 'nomare' in an archaic form). So the phrase means 'who was once called'.
segments.purgatorio-16-g154: Adverb 'francescamente' = 'in a Franciscan manner' or 'in the Franciscan style'; it modifies the following epithet and indicates the form or spirit of the name that follows (i.e. named in the simple/Franciscan manner).
segments.purgatorio-16-g155: 'il semplice Lombardo' is a noun phrase serving as the name/epithet: definite article + adjective 'semplice' ('plain/simple' here with approving/humble nuance) + gentilic 'Lombardo' (a man from Lombardy or Lombard). Together, after 'francescamente', it gives the nickname 'the simple Lombard'.
segments.purgatorio-16-g156: 'Dì oggimai' is an exhortation 'say now'—Dante uses an archaic/poetic form of the imperative of 'dire' (to say). 'che' introduces the content clause. 'la Chiesa di Roma' = 'the Church of Rome' (subject+of-phrase).
segments.purgatorio-16-g157: 'per confondere in sé due reggimenti' — 'per' here expresses cause/means ('by' or 'in order to'); 'confondere' is the infinitive 'to mix/confound'; 'in sé' = 'in herself' (reflexive: the Church taking both powers into itself); 'due reggimenti' = 'two regiments/governments' (reggimento in medieval usage = rule/government). Grammatically, an infinitival purpose/description of how the Church acts.
segments.purgatorio-16-g158: 'cade nel fango' = 'falls into the mud' — 'cade' is 3rd person singular present 'falls', 'nel' = 'in the', 'fango' = 'mud/filth' (figurative for disgrace).
segments.purgatorio-16-g159: 'e sé brutta e la soma' — 'e' = 'and'; 'sé' = reflexive 'herself'; 'brutta' here functions as an adjectival/participial predicate ('makes/has herself made ugly' or 'disfigures herself'); 'e la soma' = 'and the burden' (la soma = the load/responsibility). The construction compactly says the Church disgraces both herself and her burden.
segments.purgatorio-16-g160: A vocative address: 'O Marco mio' = 'O my Marco' (the possessive follows the name, a normal poetic placement), used to call the interlocutor affectionately.
segments.purgatorio-16-g161: 'diss’ io' is an archaic past speech form meaning 'I said' (diss' is a contracted form of 'dissi'/'dissi'' or similar). The subject pronoun 'io' is emphatic and follows the verb in speech formulae.
segments.purgatorio-16-g162: 'bene argomenti' = 'you argue well' or 'you have good arguments'. Here 'bene' is an adverb modifying 'argomenti'; 'argomenti' functions as a present indicative verbal form (you argue) or as a nominal predicate 'you make good arguments'—in either reading it praises Marco's reasoning.
segments.purgatorio-16-g163: 'e or discerno perché dal retaggio li figli di Levì furono essenti' = 'and now I discern/see why, because of the heritage, the sons of Levi were exempt.' 'discerno' is 1st person present 'I discern'; 'perché' here means 'why' (introducing the content); 'dal retaggio' = 'from the heritage/inheritance'; 'li figli di Levì' = 'the sons of Levi'; 'furono essenti' = 'were exempt/left out' (past passive sense: 'they were exempted').
segments.purgatorio-16-g164: 'Ma qual Gherardo è quel che tu per saggio di’ ch’è rimaso de la gente spenta' — 'Ma' = 'but'; 'qual' = 'which/what'; 'quel' = 'that one'; 'che' = relative 'who/that'; 'tu per saggio' = 'you, as a wise man' or 'in your capacity as a sage' (per = 'as'); 'di’' is an archaic or contracted form for 'dici'/'you say' used here; 'ch’è rimaso' = 'that has remained' (ch' = che, è = is/has, rimaso = past participle/archaic perfect of 'rimanere' = 'remained'); 'de la gente spenta' = 'of the extinguished/departed people' (gente spenta = people that have died/are gone).
segments.purgatorio-16-g165: 'in rimprovèro del secol selvaggio?' = 'as a reproach to the savage age?' — 'in' = 'in/as (a cause or capacity)', 'rimprovèro' = 'reproach' (noun), 'del secol' = 'of the age/century', 'selvaggio' = 'wild/savage' describing that age. The speaker asks whether this surviving Gherardo stands as a reproach to the rough/secular times.

sentences.purgatorio-16-s10: Thus it was said by a single voice; whereupon my master said: "Answer, and ask whether one goes up from here."
sentences.purgatorio-16-s11: And I: "O creature who are purging yourselves to become beautiful again for him who made you, you will hear marvels, if you follow me."
sentences.purgatorio-16-s12: "I will follow you as far as I am allowed," he answered; "and if seeing does not permit us, hearing will keep us joined in that stead."
sentences.purgatorio-16-s5: Are those the spirits I hear, master?
sentences.purgatorio-16-s7: And he to me: 'You, truly, understand, and from anger they go loosening the knot.'
sentences.purgatorio-16-s8: Now you — who are you that cleave our smoke, and speak of us as if you were still to leave at the calends?
sentences.purgatorio-16-s17: Thus he answered, and added, "I beg you to pray for me when you are up above."
sentences.purgatorio-16-s18: And I to him: "By my faith I bind myself to you to do what you ask of me; but I am bursting within with a doubt, if I do not explain it to myself."
sentences.purgatorio-16-s19: Formerly it was an outrage, and now in your judgment it has been made double, which convinces me here and elsewhere of that which I couple with it.
sentences.purgatorio-16-s20: The world is indeed thus entirely deserted of every virtue, as you tell me, and laden and covered with wickedness; but I beg you to point out to me the cause, so that I may see it and show it to others; for one (cause) places it in heaven, and another sets it down here below.
sentences.purgatorio-16-s33: You can clearly see that bad conduct is the cause that has made the world guilty, and not nature that is corrupted in you.
sentences.purgatorio-16-s34: Rome used to have — and it made the good world — two suns, which, the one as well as the other, showed the way both to the world and to God.
sentences.purgatorio-16-s35: Each has put the other out; and the sword has come together with the pastoral staff, and it is ill-fitting that the two should go on together by sheer force; for, once joined, one does not fear the other. If you do not believe me, pay attention to the ear of corn, for every plant is known by its seed.
sentences.purgatorio-16-s36: In the country watered by the Adige and the Po there used to be valour and courtesy, before Federigo had his troubles; now one can certainly pass onward from there whatever one may have left, out of shame to talk with the good or to draw near to them.
sentences.purgatorio-16-s1: Darkness of hell and of night deprived of every planet, under a poor sky, as much as a cloud can be dark, did not place so thick a veil over my face as that smoke which there covered us, nor — on feeling such harsh hair — could the eye endure to remain open; wherefore my escort, experienced and faithful, drew near to me and offered me his shoulder.
sentences.purgatorio-16-s2: Just as a blind man goes behind his guide so as not to lose his way and so as not to strike a bump against something that would annoy him, or perhaps even kill him, I was going along through the bitter and filthy air, listening to my duke who kept saying merely: 'Watch that you not be cut off from me.'
sentences.purgatorio-16-s3: I heard voices, and each seemed to pray for peace and for mercy to the Lamb of God who takes away their sins.
sentences.purgatorio-16-s4: Still 'Agnus Dei' were their openings; one word and one manner were in all, so that there seemed among them complete concord.
sentences.purgatorio-16-s21: A deep sigh, which pressed grief into an "uhi!"
sentences.purgatorio-16-s22: He first put it forth; and then he began: "Brother, the world is blind, and you too come from it."
sentences.purgatorio-16-s23: You who live, bring every cause up to heaven, even as if everything were moved with it by necessity.
sentences.purgatorio-16-s24: If this were so, free will would be destroyed in you, and there would be no justice — for good, there would be joy; and for evil, mourning.
sentences.purgatorio-16-s45: Look at the dawn which already begins to whiten through the smoke, and I must leave (the angel is there) before I appear to him.
sentences.purgatorio-16-s46: So he turned away, and would not listen to me any longer.
sentences.purgatorio-16-s29: At first he tastes the flavour of a little good; there he deceives himself, and runs after it, if guide or bridle do not turn aside his love.
sentences.purgatorio-16-s30: Hence it was necessary to set down law as a bridle; it was necessary to have a king who might at least distinguish the tower from the true city.
sentences.purgatorio-16-s31: The laws exist, but who will put a hand to them?
sentences.purgatorio-16-s32: No one; for the shepherd who goes before may grumble, but he does not have split claws; wherefore the people, who see their guide intent only on that good to strike which they are greedy for, feed on that and ask no more.
sentences.purgatorio-16-s42: "Either your speech deceives me, or it tests me," he answered me; "for, speaking Tuscan to me, it seems that he hears nothing of the good Gherardo."
sentences.purgatorio-16-s43: By another surname I do not know him, unless I were to take him from his daughter Gaia.
sentences.purgatorio-16-s44: God be with you; for I no longer accompany you.
sentences.purgatorio-16-s25: The heavens set your motions in motion; I do not mean all of them, but — granted that I say it — a light is given to you for good and for wickedness, and a free will; for, if toil endures in the first battles with Heaven, afterwards everything conquers, if it be well nourished.
sentences.purgatorio-16-s26: By greater force and better nature you are subject; and that (nature) creates the mind in you, which Heaven does not have in its charge.
sentences.purgatorio-16-s27: Therefore, if the present world goes astray, the cause is in you, it inclines toward you; and I will now be a true informer of it to you.
sentences.purgatorio-16-s28: It comes forth from the hand of him who longs for it before it exists, like a girl who, crying and laughing, plays childishly; the simple little soul that knows nothing, except that, moved by a joyful Maker, it willingly returns to that which amuses it.
sentences.purgatorio-16-s13: Then I began: "With that sash which death dissolves I go up less, and I came here through infernal anguish."
sentences.purgatorio-16-s14: And if God has shut me up in his grace, so that he wishes that I may see his court in a manner wholly outside of modern custom, do not hide from me who you were before death, but tell me, and tell me whether I am going the right way at the pass; and your words shall be our guides."
sentences.purgatorio-16-s15: "I was a Lombard, and I was called Marco; I knew the world, and I loved that valor to which now each has stretched the bow."
sentences.purgatorio-16-s16: To climb up, go straight ahead."
sentences.purgatorio-16-s37: There are indeed three old men still in whom the ancient age rebukes the new, and it seems slow to them that God should restore them to a better life: Currado da Palazzo and the good Gherardo and Guido da Castel, who was once called, in the Franciscan manner, the simple Lombard.
sentences.purgatorio-16-s38: Say now that the Church of Rome, by mixing two governments into herself, falls into the mud, and makes herself ugly and her burden.
sentences.purgatorio-16-s39: 'O my Marco,' I said, 'you argue well; and now I see why, from the heritage, the sons of Levi were exempt.'
sentences.purgatorio-16-s40: But which Gherardo is that whom you, as a sage, say remains of the extinguished people, as a reproach to the savage age?
